coredata.dc:title: Highly selective fluorescence chemosensor based on carbon-dot-aerogel for detection of aniline gas
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yusha
Chen, Xu
Bai, Jianliang
Yuan, Guojun
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Solid-state fluorescence sensing has become a research hotspot because it is simple and convenient in practical operation. In this study, the carbon dots (CDs)-silica aerogel solid fluorescent material was developed for the selective detection of aniline gas. The blue CDs were prepared by one-step simply hydrothermally treating low-cost sawdust, then CDs-silica aerogel composite material with the high specific surface areas and high porosity was prepared by sol-gel method based on abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs which can initiate the gelation of tetraethyl orthosilicate. It is capable of selectively distinguishing toxic gaseous aniline with other volatile organic vapors by sensitive fluorescence quenching, and under optimal conditions, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of aniline gas can reach 76.4%. It is expected that our strategy might provide a new approach for developing a low cost, fast and sensitive CDs-based probe for environmental sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Silica aerogel
Fluorescence chemosensor
Aniline gas

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs (inset: particle size distribution histogram and HRTEM image of the crystalline lattice of CDs); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths (320 nm–440 nm); (c) FT-IR spectrum of CDs (d) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s of CDs.

(a) SEM image obtained for the CDs-silica aerogel; (b) The N2 adsorption−desorption isotherms measured for the CDs-silica aerogel (inset: Pore size distribution curve for the CDs-silica aerogel); (c) FT-IR spectra of silica and CDs-silica aerogel;(d) XPS survey of CDs and CDs-silica.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of the CDs-silica aerogel (black curve) and exposed to 480 ppm aniline vapor (red curve); (b) The relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (1 − F/F0) and aniline concentration at the observed 96–1920 ppm; (inset: the linear fit graph of the relationship between fluorescence intensity and aniline gas concentration); (c) Selectivity of CDs-silica aerogel for aniline: The black bars represent the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of CDs-silica aerogel upon various toxic gaseous. The red bars represent the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of CDs-silica aerogel under the coexistence conditions of each organic molecule and aniline gas. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Reactions of TEOS with water (1) and CDs (2) [23]; (b) Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure for obtained CDs-silica aerogel.

head.ce:title: Highly selective fluorescence chemosensor based on carbon-dot-aerogel for detection of aniline gas

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 211189, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid-state fluorescence sensing has become a research hotspot because it is simple and convenient in practical operation. In this study, the carbon dots (CDs)-silica aerogel solid fluorescent material was developed for the selective detection of aniline gas. The blue CDs were prepared by one-step simply hydrothermally treating low-cost sawdust, then CDs-silica aerogel composite material with the high specific surface areas and high porosity was prepared by sol-gel method based on abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs which can initiate the gelation of tetraethyl orthosilicate. It is capable of selectively distinguishing toxic gaseous aniline with other volatile organic vapors by sensitive fluorescence quenching, and under optimal conditions, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of aniline gas can reach 76.4%. It is expected that our strategy might provide a new approach for developing a low cost, fast and sensitive CDs-based probe for environmental sensing applications.

CDs-silica aerogel composite material for the detection of aniline gas.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Aniline is an important organic chemical raw material and fine chemical intermediate. The toxicity of aniline is relatively high and only a small amount can cause poisoning. Aniline is harmful to human health which can quickly be absorbed into the human body through the skin, digestive tract and respiratory tract when exposure to such vapor, which eventually leads to liver damage and acute or chronic poisoning [1]. Accordingly, the development of sensors that can predict the presence of aniline is critical for early warning and air quality monitoring applications.

The development of nanomaterials into potential fluorescent probes has attracted significant interest in the last decade due to their advantages of simple and high efficiency, stability and high selectivity. As an emerging class of carbon nanomaterials with size less than 10 nm, carbon dots (CDs) are widely used to detect heavy metal ions in water [2,3], harmful substances and pesticide residues in food [4,5], biological cell metabolites [6], due to their unique optical properties, low toxicity, excellent water solubility and facile functionalization. At present, the most commonly employed strategy for synthesizing CDs is bottom-up route, including microwave method [7,8], pyrolysis [9], hydrothermal synthesis [10,11] and ultrasonic method [12]. Among them, hydrothermal method has been widely studied because of its simple experimental device, low energy consumption, and large-scale production possibilities. In addition, solid-state fluorescence sensing is one of the most appealing detection techniques because of its simplicity and convenience in practical operation. One of the obstacles to achieve efficient CDs phosphors is fluorescence quenching induced by their aggregation, which greatly limits the practical application of solid-state fluorescence sensing based on CDs [13,14]. The common method to avoid this undesirable effect is to place the CDs in a solid matrix such as polymer [15–17], urea [18] and silica [19,20]. Jiang et al. directly coated the prepared CDs on polyvinylidene fluoride film to produce a new probe to detect toxic gaseous ammonia [21]. Nevertheless, this method is cumbersome to operate, and the film can only be used to detect the presence of gas, while hardly remove trace gases. Rui et al. used the impregnation method to introduce the CDs prepared by branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI) and citric acid into the mesoporous silica aerogels to obtain CDs@SiO2 composite material with high fluorescence and used for the detection of NO2 gas, which showed good sensitivity and selectivity [22]. However, as the amount of immersed CDs increases, the CDs are hardly distributed evenly in the pores.

In this paper, we synthesized the blue fluorescent CDs by hydrothermal treatment of sawdust. The surface of prepared CDs contains a large amount of hydroxyl groups which can initiate the gelation of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) [23]. Based on this, high-efficiency fluorescent CDs-silica aerogel composites were prepared by one-step sol-gel method with a quantum yield of 43.4%, which not only effectively solved the solid quenching problem of CDs, but also retained the large specific surface areas and porous characteristics of the silicone gel. This method is simple and environmentally friendly. At the same time, CDs-silica aerogel is used to detect aniline gas, which can immediately and easily detect toxic aniline vapor with high selectivity and good sensitivity.

The blue fluorescent CDs were prepared by one-pot carbonization of sawdust at 200 oC. Sawdust contains cellulose and lignin, which is widespread, easily available, low-cost, and acting as the carbon precursor. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) has been used to characterize the morphology and size of the as-prepared CDs. As can be seen, TEM image shows that the CDs are spherical particles (Fig. 1a) with a size distribution within the range of 3–12 nm and an average diameter of around 7.28 nm, in good agreement with the Gaussian distribution. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1a, inset) exhibits a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm which may be attributed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene. As shown in Fig. 1b, the peak wavelength of fluorescent emission displays an excitation-dependent manner, and the CDs exhibited the maximum blue emission around 439 nm as excitation at 360 nm. The FT-IR spectroscopy of the as-prepared CDs (Fig. 1c) displays characteristic absorption bands of OH, CH, CO, CC, CN and CO at 3415, 2931, 1716, 1622, 1388 and 1097 cm−1, respectively. These results imply that the functional groups, including carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxy groups are introduced onto the surfaces of the CDs during the reaction. The XPS is performed to further analyze the composition and the surface state of the CDs. In detail, the high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1d) exhibits three peaks at 284.8, 286.4 and 287.7 eV, which are attributed to the C–C/C=C, C–O/C-N and C=O/C=N, respectively. Moreover, the high-resolution spectrum of O1s shows two peaks at 531.6 and 533.0 eV, corresponding to CO and CO, respectively (Fig. S1). The functional groups identified by the XPS are in good agreement with FT-IR.

We all know that CDs have good fluorescence properties and are one of the excellent choices for fluorescence sensor. Considering the detection state of aniline toxicants and the simplicity and convenience of solid-state fluorescent probes in practical detection applications, we plan to make solid-state fluorescent probe based on CDs. However, it is widely reported that the fluorescence of CDs will quench drastically when they are dried to powder form, because of the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching effect. Silica aerogel is an attractive porous material and has potential application in various technical fields due to its unique properties, such as high specific surface areas, high thermal insulation value, low cost and low density, which make it be the ideal carrier for detecting aniline gas. Considering that the surface of the CDs we prepared contains a large amount of hydroxyl groups, we prepared the CDs based fluorescent aerogel composite material by a simpler chemical reaction between TEOS and CDs. The gelation process of silica can be divided into two steps, commonly referred to as hydrolysis and subsequent condensation reactions. As shown in Scheme 1(a), TEOS can react with water to form intermediate 1 during the hydrolysis process, at the same time, the hydroxyl groups from the surface of the CDs can react with TEOS, resulting in the formation of intermediate product 2 [23], which has been proved that there are a large number of hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs. Therefore, the water content of the reaction mixture is very important to directly determine the degree of reaction of reactions 1 and 2. Reduction of water content in the reaction mixture during the experiment may help to inhibit the hydrolysis of TEOS and facilitate the reaction between TEOS and CDs, otherwise intermediate product 1 formation is much faster than intermediate product 2 in the presence of excess water, which leads to the conversion of TEOS to pure silica, so ethanol: water = 3.86: 1 was chosen. Compared with the blank silica gel, the doping of the CDs did reduce the gel time which further proved that the CDs reacted with TEOS (Table S1). The entire preparation process of fluorescent CDs composite aerogel is schematically illustrated in Scheme 1(b). Silica aerogel was prepared according to a previously reported method [24]. Finally, yellow transparent and light silica aerogel with blue fluorescence emission was obtained after drying the wet silica gel.

In order to investigate the morphology and structure of the synthesized CDs-silica aerogel, TEM, scanning electron microscope (SEM), Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET), FTIR and XPS were carried out. We hardly find CDs in the TEM image (Fig. S2) because CDs were coated during silica gel process for one-step preparing method of CDs-silica aerogel, and the morphology is not much different from that of the pure silica aerogel. SEM image of CDs-silica aerogel shows typical porous structures of aerogels (Fig. 2a). BET test shows that CDs-silica aerogel has a large specific surface area with 692.38 m2/g, and specifically, the nitrogen adsorption−desorption isotherms exhibit type IV adsorption (Fig. 2b). The insert of Fig. 2b shows that the pore volume and average pore diameter of the CDs-silica aerogel were 0.68 cm3/g and 3.36 nm, respectively. In a word, the BET analysis suggested that the CDs-silica aerogels were very suitable for effective vapors adsorption such as aniline gas due to its high porosity and high specific surface areas. Fig. 2c shows FT-IR spectra of CDs-silica aerogel, spectra of the two samples is very similar to the blank aerogel: The peaks at 1078 cm−1 and 798 cm−1 are attributed to the antisymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration of SiOSi respectively. The peak appearing at 3446 cm−1 corresponds to the OH antisymmetric stretching vibration, the peak appearing around 1661 cm−1 represents the OH bending vibration, and the peak appearing near 954 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of Si-OH. The XPS spectrum presented in Fig. 2d shows three typical peaks: C1s (285 eV), N1 s (399 eV) and O1s (533 eV), indicating that the CDs consist of C, N and O elements, as a control, the CDs-silica appeared Si2s and Si2p peak at the same time. In addition, as we can see from Fig. 3a, the composite has a blue fluorescence emission peak at 439 nm, which is a typical fluorescent peak of the CDs we prepared. In summary, XPS and fluorescence spectroscopy results indirect proved that the CDs are coated with silica, in other words, composite fluorescence materials have been successfully prepared.

Fig. 3a shows the variation of the fluorescence emission spectrum when CDs-silica aerogel is exposed to aniline vapor with a concentration of 480 ppm. The fluorescence spectrum clearly shows that aniline significantly quenches the emission of CDs-silica aerogel, and the characteristic fluorescence emission peak at 439 nm is not only obviously reduced, but also shifted to longer wavelengths (463 nm). The tuning of fluorescent signal is due to the adsorption of aniline on the surface and pores of the CDs-aerogels. XPS results indicated that the content of C and N element and the proportion of C-N/C-O both increased in the high resolution C1s XPS spectra, which means that aniline was adsorbed on the surface of CDs-aerogels (Fig. S3 and Table S2). The strong fluorescence quenching by aniline may attribute to the interaction of electron absorption (from the CDs-aerogels surface) and electron donating of aniline, thereby suppressing the radiation recombination of electrons and holes, resulting in observed fluorescence quenching and signal conversion [22,25,26]. In order to explore the optimal conditions for the composite aerogel used to detect aniline gas, we investigated the relationship between the concentration of CDs and the quenching efficiency of aniline gas (Fig. S4). It is clearly showed that the quenching efficiency of the CDs-silica aerogel for aniline gas increased first and then decreased as the CDs concentration increased from 0 mg·mL−1 to 25 mg·mL−1. It is well understood that when the concentration of CDs is low, the content of intermediate product 2 is quite low. And TEOS continues to react with water to form silica nanoparticles, which will gradually encapsulate intermediate product 2. As the concentration of CDs increases, the amount of intermediate product 2 increases, the formation of silica and intermediate product 2 occur simultaneously, and the gelation viscosity also increases, resulting in an embedded three-dimensional structure. However, when the concentration of CDs is higher than 12.5 mg·mL−1, the CDs will collide or aggregate accordingly, and the fluorescence intensity of the composite material is weakened, resulting in the decrease in the quenching efficiency of the aniline gas, so the optimum concentration of the CDs is 12.5 mg·mL−1. Fig. 3b shows the relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (1 − F/F0) and aniline concentration at the observed 96–1920 ppm, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of aniline gas can reach 76.4%. The inset is a linear fit graph of the relationship between fluorescence intensity and aniline gas concentration (96–960 ppm), and the limited of detection (LOD) is calculated as 77.6 ppm (R2 = 0.9802). High selectivity is important for the development of good sensors, therefore, nine other volatile organic vapors are selected, including styrene, toluene, formaldehyde, chloroform, trichloroethylene, ethyl ether, ethyl acetate, DMF and acetone. To assess whether such CDs-silica aerogel gas probe is specific for the detection of aniline, the concentrations of gases were all 960 ppm. Under the same experimental operating conditions, it can be seen from Fig. 3c that all of the interfering gases but aniline exhibited slight fluorescence quenching, revealing that the CDs-silica aerogel gas probe has good selectivity for detection of aniline gas. For real samples, many organic compounds would be coexisted. Would they adsorb on the CDs-silica aerogel and influence fluorescence? We tested the fluorescence data under the coexistence conditions of each organic molecule and aniline gas (Fig. 3c). The results showed that the presence of other gases definitely affects the quenching efficiency of aniline due to the adsorption of gas by such porous composite, but the effect is very small and within the allowable range. Compared to common methods of detecting aniline such as Direct Electrochemical method (LOD = 34.2 ppm) [27], Flow injection double amperage (LOD = 48.9 ppm) [28], it is acceptable (LOD = 77.6 ppm) and has high selectivity especially compared to previously reported literature [25,29]. In summary, the currently developed aerogel sensor has the potential to be applied to the detection of aniline gas.

In conclusion, we have demonstrated a simple, low-cost method for developing CDs-silica aerogel fluorescent material that the CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment using sawdust. Based on the rich hydroxyl groups on the surface of the obtained CDs, the gelation of TEOS can be initiated, which can not only accelerate the gelation of TEOS, shorten gelation time, but also avoid the fluorescence quenching caused by the aggregation of CDs and expand the application of solid CDs. Beyond that, this method can be operated without an expensive instrument which simplifies operations and reduces the associated costs. And the prepared CDs-silica aerogel with strong fluorescence is a promising fluorescent probe for selective detection of aniline gas because of its high specific surface areas and porosity, exhibiting good sensitivity and excellent selectivity.

We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the Nature Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province of China (BK20151408), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2242018K1G002 and KYLX16_0193) and A Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (1107047002).
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2018.12.013.
